title: Dependency manifests and lockfiles

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Dependency manifests and lockfiles; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: NIST Secure Software Development Framework; SP 800-218 v1.1; section applicable to Dependency manifests and lockfiles
sources.claim: Dependency manifests declare requested packages and constraints, while lockfiles record a resolved graph for reproducible installation in a defined tool and platform context.

sources.locator: GitHub Documentation; accessed 2026-07; section applicable to Dependency manifests and lockfiles
sources.claim: Dependency manifests declare requested packages and constraints, while lockfiles record a resolved graph for reproducible installation in a defined tool and platform context.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Dependency manifests declare requested packages and constraints, while lockfiles record a resolved graph for reproducible installation in a defined tool and platform context.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A new library is added through the package manager so both manifest intent and lockfile resolution change and are reviewed together. Your goal is to install and reproduce a project from reviewed manifest and lockfile data. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Ignore policy; Commits and history; Diffs and patch review. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Dependency manifests declare requested packages and constraints, while lockfiles record a resolved graph for reproducible installation in a defined tool and platform context. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Optional and platform dependencies, registries, transitive updates, install scripts, integrity metadata, and tool versions can still affect reproducibility and security. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Editing a manifest version is sufficient, and lockfiles are generated noise that should never be reviewed or committed.

includes: Declare direct dependencies, review lockfile changes and avoid committing installed dependency directories.
Install and reproduce a project from reviewed manifest and lockfile data.
excludes: Optional and platform dependencies, registries, transitive updates, install scripts, integrity metadata, and tool versions can still affect reproducibility and security.

outcomes: Install and reproduce a project from reviewed manifest and lockfile data.

examples.title: Dependency manifests and lockfiles: direct case
examples.situation: A new library is added through the package manager so both manifest intent and lockfile resolution change and are reviewed together.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A new library is added through the package manager so both manifest intent and lockfile resolution change and are reviewed together.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Use the ecosystem tool, review declared and resolved changes, pin the package-manager version where needed, and test the exact lockfile installed in CI.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Use the ecosystem tool, review declared and resolved changes, pin the package-manager version where needed, and test the exact lockfile installed in CI.

examples.title: Dependency manifests and lockfiles: boundary case
examples.situation: A lockfile-only security update is inspected for unexpected package or lifecycle-script changes before tests run.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A lockfile-only security update is inspected for unexpected package or lifecycle-script changes before tests run.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Optional and platform dependencies, registries, transitive updates, install scripts, integrity metadata, and tool versions can still affect reproducibility and security.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Editing a manifest version is sufficient, and lockfiles are generated noise that should never be reviewed or committed.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Install and reproduce a project from reviewed manifest and lockfile data.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Editing a manifest version is sufficient, and lockfiles are generated noise that should never be reviewed or committed.
misconceptions.correction: Use the ecosystem tool, review declared and resolved changes, pin the package-manager version where needed, and test the exact lockfile installed in CI.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Editing a manifest version is sufficient, and lockfiles are generated noise that should never be reviewed or committed.

analogy.example: A recipe and packing line can model Dependency manifests and lockfiles: declared ingredients resolve to exact supplies, controlled steps produce an artifact, and labels preserve provenance.
usefulFor: Understanding dependency resolution, configuration, repeatable builds, and generated artifacts
limitations: Software supply chains include executable dependencies, caches, signatures, platform variance, and secret handling beyond a kitchen process.
Optional and platform dependencies, registries, transitive updates, install scripts, integrity metadata, and tool versions can still affect reproducibility and security.
